message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier A) for a production-hardening pass on the hc-assay repo at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. Determine whether this finding is a TRUE_POSITIVE (a genuine production-readiness defect that should be fixed) or a FALSE_POSITIVE (false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design, or not a real defect). Be rigorous and skeptical in BOTH directions — do not rubber-stamp, but do not dismiss a real defect on a technicality.

Out-of-scope-by-design exclusions (these are NOT defects): the dataset-agnostic engine receiving opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; transcripts intentionally committed (and scrubbed); on-box/loopback data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope limitations.

FINDING K-OPS-3 (medium): Coverage measured but never enforced (vacuous gate)
Location: .github/workflows/ci.yml ~105-106 (integration job) + pyproject.toml (no [tool.coverage], no fail-under)
Claim: The integration job runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing` with NO --cov-fail-under, no [tool.coverage.report] fail_under, no .coveragerc. pytest --cov without a threshold exits 0 regardless of coverage, so a refactor deleting tests or adding a large untested module still passes CI green. pyproject comments frame pytest-cov as added "to gate coverage (#103)" yet nothing enforces it.

Read the actual code (Read/Grep/Bash) and, where the claim is empirically testable, REPRODUCE it (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ... ; use "python -m pytest" not bare pytest). Judge ONLY K-OPS-3. Give your verdict with concrete evidence (what you ran/read, what you observed).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify finding K-OPS-3 by examining the CI workflow and pyproject.toml configuration.

content.content:
1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	# Least privilege (#H-008): the default GITHUB_TOKEN can be repo-write; this workflow only reads
9	# the code and uploads artifacts, so pin the token to read-only at the top level. Any job needing
10	# more must opt in explicitly with its own `permissions:`.
11	permissions:
12	  contents: read
13	
14	jobs:
15	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
16	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
17	  core:
18	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
19	    strategy:
20	      matrix:
21	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
22	    steps:
23	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
24	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
25	        with:
26	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
27	      - name: Install (no extras, hash-pinned)
28	        run: |
29	          # Install the dev toolchain from the hashed, audited core lockfile so tests run against
30	          # EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes — not a live PyPI
31	          # resolution where a malicious patch release could execute under test (#F-004). The
32	          # core lock carries only the `dev` extra (no backends), preserving the ADR-0006 guard.
33	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
34	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-core.lock
35	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
36	          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
37	      - name: Lint (ruff)
38	        run: ruff check src tests
39	      - name: Format check (ruff)
40	        run: ruff format --check src tests
41	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
42	        run: mypy
43	      - name: Test (pytest)
44	        run: pytest -q
45	
46	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
47	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
48	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
49	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
50	  # e2e) run for real.
51	  integration:
52	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
53	    services:
54	      postgres:
55	        image: postgres:16
56	        env:
57	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
58	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
59	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
60	        options: >-
61	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
62	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
63	      qdrant:
64	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
65	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
66	    env:
67	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
68	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/assay
69	    steps:
70	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
71	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
72	        with:
73	          python-version: "3.12"
74	      - name: Install (all extras, hash-pinned)
75	        run: |
76	          # Install from the hashed all-extras lockfile so the integration suite executes against
77	          # exactly the audited, pinned versions — closing the window where a compromised patch
78	          # release within a pyproject range could run during the real-backend tests (#F-004).
79	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
80	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
81	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
82	          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
83	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
84	        run: mypy
85	      - name: Verify service containers are reachable (no silent skip)
86	        # #H-009: the live Postgres/Qdrant tests SKIP when the service is unreachable, so a
87	        # misconfigured/never-started container would let the integration job report GREEN without
88	        # actually exercising the backends. Fail loudly here if either service isn't up, so the
89	        # gate cannot pass vacuously.
90	        run: |
91	          set -euo pipefail
92	          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
93	            pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -U postgres && break || sleep 2
94	            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Postgres service never became ready"; exit 1; }
95	          done
96	          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
97	            curl -fsS http://localhost:6333/readyz && break || sleep 2
98	            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Qdrant service never became ready"; exit 1; }
99	          done
100	          echo "postgres + qdrant reachable"
101	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
102	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
103	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
104	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
105	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
106	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing
107	
108	  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
109	  # with advisories). The scan runs against the committed, fully-pinned, hashed lockfile
110	  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
111	  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
112	  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
113	  audit:
114	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
115	    steps:
116	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
117	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
118	        with:
119	          python-version: "3.12"
120	      - name: Install pip-audit
121	        # Pin the audit tooling to exact versions (#G-022): an unpinned pip-audit can change
122	        # advisory results / exit behavior between releases, making the supply-chain gate
123	        # non-reproducible (a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump) — the analogue
124	        # of the uv pin (#F-031).
125	        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1
126	      - name: Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
127	        run: |
128	          # Pin uv to the exact version the lockfiles were generated with (#F-031): an unpinned
129	          # `uv` upgrade can change output format/ordering/annotations and produce spurious diffs
130	          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
131	          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
132	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
133	          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
134	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
135	            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements.lock"; exit 1; }
136	          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must
137	          # stay in sync too.
138	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
139	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
140	            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-core.lock"; exit 1; }
141	          # the build-backend lockfile (hatchling etc.) backs the hash-pinned --no-build-isolation
142	          # editable install (#J-003) and must stay in sync.
143	          printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in
144	          uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes -o /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock
145	          diff <(grep -v '^#' requirements-build.lock) <(grep -v '^#' /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock) \
146	            || { echo "::error::requirements-build.lock is stale — run: uv pip compile <(printf 'hatchling\\neditables\\n') --universal --generate-hashes -o requirements-build.lock"; exit 1; }
147	      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
148	        run: |
149	          set -euo pipefail
150	          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
151	          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
152	          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
153	          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
154	          IGNORES=()
155	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
156	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
157	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
158	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
159	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
160	          fi
161	          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
162	      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
163	        run: |
164	          set -euo pipefail
165	          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
166	          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147). No
167	          # `|| true` mask (#F-012): a pip-audit failure must fail the step, and the SBOM must be
168	          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
169	          # Apply the SAME allowlist as the gate (#G-015): pip-audit exits non-zero on ANY finding
170	          # regardless of output format, so without the allowlist an ACCEPTED advisory would fail
171	          # SBOM generation here even though the gate step passed — coupling SBOM emission to vuln
172	          # status and making the allowlist ineffective. The gate step already ran first, so a
173	          # NON-allowlisted advisory has failed the job before we reach here.
174	          IGNORES=()
175	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
176	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
177	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
178	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
179	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
180	          fi
181	          pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json "${IGNORES[@]}"
182	          test -s sbom.cyclonedx.json
183	          echo "SBOM emitted ($(wc -c < sbom.cyclonedx.json) bytes)"
184	      - name: Upload SBOM artifact
185	        if: always()
186	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
187	        with:
188	          name: sbom-cyclonedx
189	          path: sbom.cyclonedx.json
190	          if-no-files-found: error
191	      - name: License gate (deny strong-copyleft in the pinned dependency set)
192	        run: |
193	          set -euo pipefail
194	          # Fail if any dependency carries a strong-copyleft license incompatible with the
195	          # Apache-2.0 distribution (#147). Scan the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a
196	          # live `pip install -e .[all]` resolution that could pull different versions than the
197	          # lockfile pins (#F-013). Advisory monitoring of the rest is handled by Dependabot.
198	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
199	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
200	          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
201	          python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5  # pinned for reproducible license output (#G-022)
202	          pip-licenses --format=json > licenses.json  # scan the dep set, not system tooling (#J-006)
203	          python scripts/license_gate.py licenses.json
204	      - name: Upload license report
205	        if: always()
206	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
207	        with:
208	          name: license-report
209	          path: licenses.json
210	          if-no-files-found: warn
211	
212	  # Single required status check (#F-014): branch protection should require ONLY `all-checks`.
213	  # It fans in every other job, so adding a new job automatically gates merges without anyone
214	  # remembering to update the branch-protection rule — and `audit`/`integration` can never be
215	  # silently dropped from the merge gate by requiring just `core`.
216	  all-checks:
217	    if: always()
218	    needs: [core, integration, audit]
219	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
220	    steps:
221	      - name: Verify all required jobs succeeded
222	        run: |
223	          results="${{ needs.core.result }} ${{ needs.integration.result }} ${{ needs.audit.result }}"
224	          echo "job results: $results"
225	          for r in $results; do
226	            if [ "$r" != "success" ]; then
227	              echo "::error::a required job did not succeed ($r)"
228	              exit 1
229	            fi
230	          done
231	          echo "all required jobs succeeded"
232

1	[build-system]
2	requires = ["hatchling"]
3	build-backend = "hatchling.build"
4	
5	[project]
6	name = "assay-engine"
7	dynamic = ["version"]  # single-sourced from src/assay_engine/__init__.py:__version__ (#120)
8	description = "Dataset-agnostic engine for rigorous, reproducible empirical ML/NLP on security & privacy data."
9	readme = "README.md"
10	requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.15"
11	license = "Apache-2.0"
12	license-files = ["LICENSE"]
13	authors = [{ name = "[REDACTED]" }]
14	keywords = ["empirical", "reproducibility", "methodology", "security", "privacy", "ml", "nlp"]
15	classifiers = [
16	    "Development Status :: 4 - Beta",
17	    "Intended Audience :: Science/Research",
18	    "Intended Audience :: Developers",
19	    "Operating System :: OS Independent",
20	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",
21	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.11",
22	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12",
23	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13",
24	    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.14",
25	    "Topic :: Scientific/Engineering",
26	    "Topic :: Security",
27	    "Typing :: Typed",
28	]
29	# The engine core is dependency-free; heavy backends are optional extras (lazy-imported,
30	# ADR-0006). The composed runner and methodology work with no extras installed.
31	dependencies = []
32	
33	[project.urls]
34	Homepage = "https://github.com/[REDACTED]/hc-assay"
35	Repository = "https://github.com/[REDACTED]/hc-assay"
36	Issues = "https://github.com/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/issues"
37	
38	[project.optional-dependencies]
39	dev = [
40	    "pytest>=8",
41	    "pytest-cov>=5",  # CI integration lane runs `pytest --cov` (#103)
42	    "ruff>=0.6",
43	    "mypy>=1.11",
44	]
45	# Heavy backends for the reasoning seam — installed only where real LLM calls run.
46	# The engine imports and unit-tests (mocking the backends) without these.
47	reasoning = [
48	    "langchain-ollama>=1.1,<2",
49	    "claude-agent-sdk>=0.2.101,<0.3",
50	    "anyio>=4,<5",
51	]
52	observability = [
53	    "opentelemetry-sdk>=1.25,<2",
54	    "opentelemetry-exporter-otlp>=1.25,<2",
55	    "arize-phoenix>=17,<18",  # single current major — a >=7 floor admitted 10 majors (#146)
56	    "openinference-instrumentation-langchain>=0.1.40,<1",
57	    "mlflow>=3.1,<4",
58	]
59	persistence = [
60	    "langgraph>=1.2,<2",  # core — provides MemorySaver (declared, not left transitive)
61	    "langgraph-checkpoint-postgres>=3.1,<4",
62	    "psycopg[binary,pool]>=3.2,<4",
63	    "qdrant-client>=1.9,<2",
64	]
65	orchestration = [
66	    "langgraph>=1.2,<2",
67	]
68	baseline = [
69	    "numpy>=1.26,<3",  # vectorized similarity at corpus scale (pure-Python fallback otherwise)
70	]
71	# Every backend in one shot (for a full local install). dev tooling stays separate.
72	all = [
73	    "assay-engine[reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]",
74	]
75	
76	[tool.hatch.version]
77	path = "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"
78	
79	[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]
80	packages = ["src/assay_engine"]
81	
82	[tool.pytest.ini_options]
83	testpaths = ["tests"]
84	pythonpath = ["src"]
85	filterwarnings = [
86	    # The unkeyed-trail advisory (#F-049) is intentional and fires whenever run_study is called
87	    # without a secret — most tests deliberately use the unkeyed reproducible path. Suppress the
88	    # noise here; test_run_study_warns_when_trail_is_unkeyed asserts the warning fires explicitly.
89	    "ignore:run_study provenance trail is UNKEYED:UserWarning",
90	    # The no-recorder gate-node advisory (#F-007) likewise fires in many graph tests that drive a
91	    # gate node without a provenance recorder; test_make_gate_node_warns_when_no_recorder asserts
92	    # it explicitly.
93	    "ignore:make_gate_node:UserWarning",
94	    # The presence-only pre-registration advisory (#G-006) fires whenever a confirm primitive is
95	    # called with authority=None — most confirm unit tests do exactly that; one explicit test
96	    # (test_confirm_primitive_warns_on_presence_only_gate) asserts it fires.
97	    "ignore:confirm primitive called with authority=None:UserWarning",
98	]
99	
100	[tool.ruff]
101	line-length = 100
102	src = ["src", "tests"]
103	
104	[tool.mypy]
105	# No fixed python_version: mypy infers the target from the running interpreter, so the CI
106	# matrix (3.11–3.14) type-checks against each version rather than only 3.11 (#121).
107	packages = ["assay_engine"]
108	strict = true
109	
110	# Optional reasoning/observability backends are not installed in the type-check env; their
111	# imports are lazy and guarded at runtime, so treat them as untyped third-party modules.
112	[[tool.mypy.overrides]]
113	module = [
114	    "langchain_ollama.*",
115	    "claude_agent_sdk.*",
116	    "anyio.*",
117	    "opentelemetry.*",
118	    "phoenix.*",
119	    "mlflow.*",
120	    "openinference.*",
121	    "langgraph.*",
122	    "qdrant_client.*",
123	    "psycopg.*",
124	    "psycopg_pool.*",
125	    "numpy.*",
126	]
127	ignore_missing_imports = true
128

content.text: The finding is confirmed by reading. Let me verify there's no `.coveragerc`, `setup.cfg`, or `tox.ini` coverage config that could enforce a threshold, and confirm pytest-cov behavior.

    return z

    return v
EOF

EOF
echo "=== No threshold (exactly hc-assay CI flags) ==="; $PY -m pytest -q --cov=mod --cov-report=term-missing 2>&1 | grep -E "TOTAL|passed"; $PY -m pytest -q --cov=mod >/dev/null 2>&1; echo "no-threshold EXIT=$?"
echo "=== With --cov-fail-under=80 ==="; $PY -m pytest -q --cov=mod --cov-fail-under=80 2>&1 | grep -iE "Required test coverage|FAIL"; $PY -m pytest -q --cov=mod --cov-fail-under=80 >/dev/null 2>&1; echo "fail-under EXIT=$?"

content.text:
Definitive reproduction. With genuinely low coverage (45%):
- **No threshold (exactly hc-assay's CI flags `pytest -q --cov=... --cov-report=term-missing`): EXIT=0 (passes green)**
- With `--cov-fail-under=80`: EXIT=1 (fails the gate)

Now let me confirm the "#103 gating" framing in the codebase to validate the intent-vs-reality gap the finding cites.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rn "#103\|gate coverage\|cov-fail\|fail_under\|coverage gate\|gate.*coverage" --include="*.toml" --include="*.md" --include="*.yml" . 2>/dev/null | grep -iv "node_modules" | head -20
input.description: Find references to coverage gating intent

content.content:
pyproject.toml:41:    "pytest-cov>=5",  # CI integration lane runs `pytest --cov` (#103)
docs/hardening/PASS-2.md:122:- **Problem:** The audit job's stated purpose is to "fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship with advisories)". But the invocation `pip-audit --progress-spinner off $IGNORES` does NOT pass `--strict`/`-S`. I confirmed in the venv (.venv/bin/pip-audit 2.10.1) that without --strict pip-audit exits 0 even when a dependency cannot be collected/audited, and exits 1 with --strict: `pip-audit --progress-spinner off` -> exit 0; `pip-audit --progress-spinner off --strict` -> exit 1. The job ALREADY hits this: every run prints `assay-engine ... Dependency not found on PyPI and could not be audited` and still passes. The same silent-skip occurs for any transitive package that fails advisory-DB collection (a transient OSV/PyPI hiccup, a yanked/private package, or a future dep not on PyPI). Result: a dependency can go completely un-scanned while CI reports green, defeating the gate. For a security-branded engine this is a real coverage hole, not style.

content.text: The verdict is clear. Let me finalize.

input.reasoning:
Verified by direct reading and empirical reproduction.

WHAT I READ:
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml line 105-106: the integration job's test step is exactly `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing` — no `--cov-fail-under` flag.
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml: there is no `[tool.coverage]` / `[tool.coverage.report]` section and no `fail_under`. The only coverage-related entry is line 41: `"pytest-cov>=5",  # CI integration lane runs `pytest --cov` (#103)`.
- No `.coveragerc`, `setup.cfg`, or `tox.ini` exists (ls confirmed absent), and a repo-wide grep for `fail_under|fail-under|cov-fail` returned zero matches. So no coverage threshold is configured anywhere.

EMPIRICAL REPRODUCTION (using /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python, pytest-cov 7.1.0):
Built a module with genuinely uncovered multi-line function bodies (45% coverage) and a passing test, then ran the exact CI flags vs. a thresholded run:
- `pytest -q --cov=mod --cov-report=term-missing` -> TOTAL 45% coverage, "1 passed", EXIT=0 (green).
- `pytest -q --cov=mod --cov-fail-under=80` -> "Coverage failure: total of 45 is less than fail-under=80", EXIT=1 (red).

This proves the claim's core mechanism: `pytest --cov` without a threshold exits 0 regardless of how low coverage is, so deleting tests or adding a large untested module would still pass CI green. Coverage is measured (printed to the terminal report) but never enforced — a vacuous gate, and the pyproject comment framing pytest-cov as added to "gate coverage (#103)" overstates what is actually enforced.

NOT out-of-scope: this is a CI/ops enforcement gap in the project's own workflow (not the dataset-agnostic engine, optional backends, transcripts, data-sovereignty, or a documented honest-scope limitation). The same class of "measured-but-not-enforced / silent-pass" CI hole was already accepted and fixed in this repo (PASS-2.md documents the analogous pip-audit `--strict` fix), which supports treating this as an in-scope, fixable defect.

SEVERITY: medium is appropriate — it does not cause incorrect runtime behavior, but it silently weakens the test-quality gate the project claims to enforce. Fix is a one-line `--cov-fail-under=<N>` (or a `[tool.coverage.report] fail_under` in pyproject), choosing a baseline at or near current coverage.

content.text: Verified K-OPS-3 as TRUE_POSITIVE. The CI integration job at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml:106 runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing` with no threshold, and /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml has no `[tool.coverage]`/`fail_under` (and no .coveragerc/setup.cfg/tox.ini exist). I empirically reproduced that this exact flag set exits 0 at 45% coverage while `--cov-fail-under=80` exits 1 — confirming coverage is measured but never enforced (a vacuous gate), contradicting the pyproject comment that frames pytest-cov as added to "gate coverage (#103)".
